items.new_candidate: Russian Whitaker [unverified — likely Whisper rendering of a first-name + 'Whitaker' surname, but canonical first name not established]
items.evidence: Context in the transcript places this person among the Mileston Fourteen / canonical Mileston-area Black landowner farmers ('they owned their own, despite not in the Mileston community. They had Russian, Whitaker, James Boyne, all of them'). External archival cross-checks against the SNCC Digital Gateway entry on Hartman Turnbow (snccdigital.org), the Mississippi Encyclopedia entry on the Holmes County Civil Rights Movement, the MS Civil Rights Project Holmes page, Carthan Enterprises' Mileston voter-registration article, and the CRM Veterans Holmes County PDF (Got to Thinking: Holmes Co. Mississippi) yield NO Whitaker among the documented First Fourteen. Documented First-14 names that recur across sources: Hartman Turnbow, Ozell Mitchell, Annie Bell Mitchell, Alma Mitchell Carnegie, Ben Square, Ralthus Hayes, Bernice Patton Montgomery, Caladonia Davis. Whitaker may have been a later Mileston-area movement participant rather than one of the First 14 — but I cannot confirm a 'Russell Whitaker' from independent archives. Per Smithsonian-grade discipline, the literal Whisper rendering should be retained with an [unverified] flag rather than canonicalized as 'Russell Whitaker' on phonetic guess alone.

items.new_candidate: Saigon Pagoda [unverified — likely Whisper rendering of a small Holmes County country church name, but canonical name not established]
items.evidence: Transcript context: Walter Bruce describes a wooden country church in the Durant/West/Goodman 'Hill towns' area where SNCC organizers came for weekly meetings; one night, night riders attempted arson with a gasoline-soaked rope tied around the church. External searches across Mississippi-United-Methodist Church directories, Holmes County Genealogy Network (holmes.msghn.org), Mississippi Civil Rights Project Holmes timeline, SNCC Digital Gateway, and CRM Veterans Holmes County document return NO church name matching 'Saigon Pagoda' or 'Salem Pagoda' phonetically. The well-documented 'Sanctified Church in Mileston' (SNCC's John Ball-led citizenship-school site) is in the Delta, not the Hill towns where Walter Bruce was organizing. 'Second Pilgrim Rest M.B. Church' in Durant is documented as Bruce's home-base community but is a poor phonetic match for 'Saigon Pagoda'. Per Smithsonian-grade discipline, the literal Whisper rendering should be retained with an [unverified] flag.

items.whisper: Reverend Mr. M. Crawford / Reverend Crawford
items.new_candidate: Reverend [surname unverified] [Whisper unstable: rendered as 'Reverend Mr. M. Crawford', 'Reverend Nick Craw', 'Reverend Crawford' in different passages]
items.evidence: Transcript context: the speaker is identifying a Black candidate (a minister) who ran in the 1967 Holmes County independent slate alongside Robert Clark and helped pull together support across communities. External archival cross-checks against the Mississippi Encyclopedia Robert G. Clark Jr. entry, SNCC Digital Gateway 'Robert Clark wins election in Holmes County', CRM Veterans Holmes County PDF, and the BlackPast.org / Mississippi Free Press obituaries enumerate the documented 1967 Holmes County Black independent candidate slate: Robert Clark, Burrell Tate, Griffin McLaurin Jr., Thomas C. 'Top Cat' Johnson, Ed Noel McGaw Jr., Ward Montgomery, John Malone, Willie James Burns, John Daniel Wesley, Ralthus Hayes, and Robert Smith. NO 'Crawford' appears in any source. The well-documented Holmes County clergy figure of the era is Rev. J. J. Russell (Erma Russell's husband), but the surname phonology does not align with 'Crawford'. Whisper's three inconsistent renderings of the same reference (123.19 = 'Reverend Mr. M. Crawford' AND 'Reverend Crawford'; 123.P2.39 = 'Reverend Nick Craw / Reverend Mr. M. Crawford') indicate the Whisper acoustic was garbled — possibly McGaw, McLaurin, or some other name with a velar consonant cluster. Per Smithsonian-grade discipline, the literal Whisper rendering should be retained with an [unverified] flag rather than canonicalized as 'Rev. Crawford'.

items.original_candidate: Rolling Fork (Whisper of Holmes County community name)
items.new_candidate: Rolling Wall / Roe Wall [unverified — small Holmes County community name; NOT Rolling Fork]
items.evidence: The Pass 2 candidate 'Rolling Fork' is REJECTED with high confidence: Rolling Fork is the seat of Sharkey County, Mississippi — approximately 60+ miles southwest of Holmes County in the Delta, NOT in Holmes County. Walter Bruce explicitly says 'Yes, I was in a little community called Rolling Wall out in the country' as his birthplace in Holmes County, and the interviewer in a separate exchange uses 'Rosewater' (also possibly a Whisper artifact). External cross-checks against the complete list of Holmes County communities (Wikipedia, MS Encyclopedia, MS Holmes County genealogy network, FamilySearch wiki, hometownlocator) — Acona, Brozville, Coxburg, Cruger, Durant, Ebenezer, Egypt, Eulogy, Franklin, Good Hope, Goodman, Gwin, Howard, Ituma, Lexington, Marcella, Mileston, Montgomery, Oregon, Owens Wells, Pickens, Pluto, Quofaloma, Richland, Tchula, Thornton, Tolarville, West — yield NO match for any of the Whisper renderings ('Rolling Wall', 'Roe Wall', 'Rosewater'). This is likely a very small unincorporated settlement or local-name-only locality without a documented modern toponym. Per Smithsonian-grade discipline, the literal Whisper rendering should be retained with an [unverified] flag; Rolling Fork as a canonical correction would be factually incorrect.

items.new_candidate: Russian Whitaker [unverified — see row 123.8 evidence; reinforcing row, same conclusion]
items.evidence: This is the Pass 2 reinforcing row of Pass 1 item 123.8. Same external-source evidence applies: no Whitaker is documented among the Mileston Fourteen / First 14 voter-registration applicants in the SNCC Digital Gateway, Mississippi Encyclopedia, MS Civil Rights Project, Carthan Enterprises, or CRM Veterans Holmes County archive. The Pass 2 confidence reinforcement does not establish external verification — both passes are derived from the same garbled Whisper acoustic. Per Smithsonian-grade discipline: retain literal Whisper rendering with [unverified] flag rather than canonicalize 'Russell Whitaker' on phonetic guess alone.

items.original_candidate: Salem Pagoda / a Holmes County church
items.new_candidate: Saigon Pagoda [unverified — see row 123.16 evidence; reinforcing row, same conclusion]
items.evidence: This is the Pass 2 reinforcing row of Pass 1 item 123.16. Same external-source evidence applies: no Holmes County Black church named 'Saigon Pagoda', 'Salem Pagoda', or any phonetically similar canonical name is documented in MS-UMC directories, Holmes County Genealogy Network, SNCC Digital Gateway, Mississippi Civil Rights Project, or CRM Veterans. The 'Salem Pagoda' candidate is a Pass-2 speculative guess; 'Salem' is a plausible Mississippi Black-church first word but the second word 'Pagoda' is non-canonical for Black-church naming conventions. The well-documented Sanctified Church in Mileston (Delta) and Second Pilgrim Rest M.B. Church in Durant are both phonetically poor matches for 'Saigon Pagoda'. Retain literal Whisper rendering with [unverified] flag.

items.whisper: Reverend Nick Craw / Reverend Mr. M. Crawford
items.new_candidate: Reverend [surname unverified] [see row 123.19 evidence; reinforcing row, same conclusion]
items.evidence: This is the Pass 2 reinforcing row of Pass 1 item 123.19. Same external-source evidence applies: the documented 1967 Holmes County Black independent candidate slate (Robert Clark, Burrell Tate, Griffin McLaurin Jr., Thomas C. 'Top Cat' Johnson, Ed Noel McGaw Jr., Ward Montgomery, John Malone, Willie James Burns, John Daniel Wesley, Ralthus Hayes, Robert Smith) contains NO 'Crawford'. Rev. J. J. Russell is the canonical documented Holmes County minister-activist but the surname does not phonetically align with 'Crawford'. Whisper's three garbled renderings of the same reference (across both Pass 1 and Pass 2) indicate acoustic uncertainty. Retain literal Whisper rendering with [unverified] flag.

items.new_candidate: [item misattributed — string does not appear in this transcript]
items.evidence: Grep verification against both the raw Whisper transcript (transcripts/raw/Walter Bruce_interview_20250705_022102/) and the corrected transcript (transcripts/corrected/Walter Bruce_interview_20250705_022102/) confirms that NEITHER 'Tony' NOR 'Zivolich' NOR 'Zivkovich' NOR 'Teamster' appears anywhere in the Walter Bruce interview text. This Pass 2 entry is a misattribution from another entry's audit — the slice note itself acknowledges 'context here is N/A; not in this transcript actually'. There is nothing in entry 123 to adjudicate; the item should be removed from the entry 123 cleanup overlay (and possibly relocated to its originating entry's audit, if traceable). The Tony Zivkovich correction (Teamsters Atlanta business agent) may well be correct for whatever entry it actually came from, but it is not applicable to Walter Bruce / entry 123.
